div.h1: Lost Update Without Mutex — buffer_data_len Race Condition

p.em: Two CPUs interleave on shared state, breaking the FIFO invariant

g.text: Scenario A: Writer (CPU 0) vs Reader (CPU 1) — Lost Update
Scenario B: CLEAR (CPU 0) vs Read (CPU 1) — Torn / Half-Zeroed Buffer
✅ THE FIX: mutex_lock_interruptible() Makes Operations Sequential

div.h2: The One Rule That Prevents Both Scenarios
div.p: Never call wait_event_interruptible() while holding mydevice_mutex. Sleep → outside the lock. Modify state → inside the lock. Wake → inside the lock (safe).

wait_event_interruptible(queue, condition) ← OUTSIDE mutex mutex_lock_interruptible(&mydevice_mutex) ← acquire AFTER waking ... copy_from/to_user, update buffer_data_len ... wake_up_interruptible(other_queue) ← INSIDE critical section mutex_unlock(&mydevice_mutex) ← release
Time Flow → (Steps executed top-to-bottom; interleaving creates the bug)

buffer_data_len = 0 ← says "empty" kernel_buffer[50..60) contains 10 bytes ← data exists bytes_used=0 but data is unaccounted for Next write overwrites the orphaned bytes silently. Next read returns EOF even though bytes were written.
Reader receives 4096 bytes that are half-zero, half-original. No crash. No error. Checksums fail. Protocol parsers corrupt. The bug is silent — hardest class of concurrency bug.

Outside every critical section: buffer_data_len ≤ buffer_size kernel_buffer[0..buffer_data_len) → valid FIFO data kernel_buffer[buffer_data_len..buffer_size) → free space No torn reads. No orphaned writes. No lost updates.
reg0 = buffer_data_len reg0 = 50 (reads from shared memory)
Copies 10 new bytes into kernel_buffer[50..60) (memory is correct)
Sees buffer_data_len = 0 10 bytes written to buffer are NOW UNREACHABLE — orphaned data
reg1 = buffer_data_len reg1 = 50 (reads stale value — CPU 0 has not stored yet)
Reads 50 bytes assuming 50 bytes are valid. (correct at this instant)
OVERWRITES CPU 0's store of 60 buffer_data_len = 0 CPU 0's 10 new bytes are now invisible to all readers
Bytes 0..2047 → all zeros (already cleared) Bytes 2048..4095 → original data (not yet cleared) Reader sees a torn mix of zeros and real data!
